agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $1.08M Berth 7 dock repair contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with Dynamo Construction for repairs to the Berth 7 dock at the city's port terminal. If approved, the contract would cost up to $1,080,650 (including a $250,000 contingency) and run until December 30, 2027. The work involves fixing a failed structural beam at the southern limit of Berth 7, including installation of support piles, concrete piers, and deck repairs. Dynamo Construction was the lowest responsive bidder at $830,650 base bid, with three other bids ranging from $998,000 to $2,973,000. The project is funded through the Port Enterprise Fund Capital Improvement Program budget.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to adopt 2025 California Fire Code with local changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on adopting the 2025 California Fire Code and the 2025 California Wildland-Urban Interface Code, with local amendments specific to Richmond. The state codes took effect January 1, 2026. Local amendments address Richmond's high seismic zone (Hayward Fault), dry summer periods (July–October), and narrow winding streets. Appendix chapters adopted include B, C, D, F, H, I, J, K, and P. If approved, these updated fire safety rules would replace the current fire prevention code in the Richmond Municipal Code. The vote is scheduled for a second reading of the ordinance, meaning it has been introduced once and now needs final approval to become law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, review the new fire code requirements for your property or business.

agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a resolution that would order the preparation of an annual Engineer's Report for the Marina Bay Landscape and Lighting Maintenance District (MBLLMD) for Fiscal Year 2026–2027. This report would detail proposed improvements and costs for maintaining landscaping and lighting in the Marina Bay area. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $1.25M road striping contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with Chrisp Company for on-call city-wide roadway striping, stenciling, and street sign services. If approved, the contract would run for an initial three-year term starting April 7, 2026, with an option to extend for two additional one-year terms. The total amount would not exceed $1.25 million, with a yearly cap of $250,000. The contractor will post 'NO PARKING' signs at least 72 hours in advance of work, and signs must be placed no more than 50 feet apart. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Fund for Children and Youth renewal up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on renewing the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth (RFCY) for a ten-year term (FY 2028-29 through FY 2037-38). If renewed without amendment, the fund will continue receiving 3% of unrestricted General Fund revenues, estimated at $99 million over the ten-year period. Council must act by December 31, 2027. The fund supports services for children, youth, and transitional-aged young adults (up to age 24) in Richmond and unincorporated North Richmond.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from the public — the council vote is the final step. Watch for the council's decision.

key_dates.label: RFCY renewal deadline for council action — Richmond

Dear City Clerk,

Please include the following public comment for the March 24 City Council meeting regarding the reauthorization of the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth (RFCY):

Good evening Mayor Martinez and Councilmembers,
My name is Danielle Ross, and I am the County Director with Fresh Lifelines for Youth (FLY), where we provide legal education, mentorship, and leadership development for system-involved young people.

Through RFCY, FLY delivers our Law Program and STAY FLY in Richmond. These programs equip young people with knowledge of their rights, build supportive relationships, and promote positive decision-making. This funding is critical, particularly as state and federal funding sources continue to contract and create instability for youth-serving organizations.

We serve young people across Richmond in schools and community-based settings, many of whom are navigating system involvement, school disengagement, and other barriers to stability. RFCY allows us to provide consistent support where it is most needed.

Without this funding, we would face difficult decisions about scaling back programming, reducing supplies, and potentially limiting staff capacity, directly impacting the number of young people we can serve.

Our programs contribute to increased safety, stronger community connections, and meaningful collaboration with schools and community-based partners, aligning closely with RFCY’s purpose and long-standing impact.

Reauthorizing RFCY is essential to sustaining this work and continuing to invest in the wellbeing and future of Richmond’s young people.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,
Danielle Ross

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to limit city resources for federal immigration enforcement
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a bylaw amendment that explicitly prohibits using city property—including buildings, parks, parking lots, vehicles, and vacant lots—as staging areas, processing locations, or operational bases for federal civil immigration enforcement. City personnel must also be trained on the new rules. If approved on first reading April 7, 2026, second reading is April 28, 2026, and the ordinance takes effect May 28, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decision; if passed, city staff and contractors must comply with new property-use restrictions and training requirements by May 28, 2026.
